metadata.description: General non-negotiation anchoring pairs for probing only. Tests whether the anchoring vector generalises beyond negotiation contexts to everyday situations involving reference points and framing effects.
metadata.note: Contexts deliberately avoid negotiation, pricing, contracts, and business deals.

dimensions.name: Anchoring
dimensions.description: Setting advantageous reference points early; framing a problem or estimate before others establish their own

pairs.context: A teacher is asking students to estimate how long a research project will take before discussing timelines.
pairs.positive: Before we hear estimates, I'll offer a benchmark: similar projects in previous years typically took six to eight weeks. Use that as your starting reference.
pairs.negative: Let's hear your estimates first. What does everyone think is realistic?

pairs.context: A committee is deciding how many volunteers are needed for an event and the chair speaks first.
pairs.positive: I'd suggest we start from the assumption we need twenty volunteers as a minimum. That's based on last year's headcount. We can argue it down if people think that's too many.
pairs.negative: How many volunteers do people think we need? Let's get a feel for the range before we decide.

pairs.context: A doctor is setting expectations before a patient asks how long recovery from a procedure will take.
pairs.positive: Most patients are back to normal activity in about six weeks. That's the typical baseline. Some are quicker, a few take longer, but plan for six.
pairs.negative: Recovery time varies a lot. Tell me about your lifestyle and I'll give you a rough idea.

pairs.context: A project manager is about to ask team members to estimate their task completion times.
pairs.positive: As a reference point, I'd expect each of these tasks to take about two days based on what I've seen on similar work. Let me know if your assessment is different.
pairs.negative: How long does everyone think each task will take? Let's go around the room.

pairs.context: An organiser is asking attendees to suggest a donation amount at a charity event.
pairs.positive: Most of our supporters tonight have contributed around £50. That's a helpful benchmark if you're not sure what's appropriate.
pairs.negative: Please give whatever you feel comfortable giving. There's no set amount — it's entirely up to you.

pairs.context: A tutor is giving feedback on an essay before a student can form their own assessment.
pairs.positive: I'll frame my feedback by saying this is a solid B+ piece. It has real strengths but specific areas that need work before it reaches A standard.
pairs.negative: Tell me what you think of the essay first and then I'll share my assessment.

pairs.context: Someone is asking their group how long they should spend on a decision before making it.
pairs.positive: I'd suggest we give ourselves fifteen minutes maximum. That's usually enough to surface the key arguments without going in circles.
pairs.negative: How long do people think we need? Let's agree on a time limit.

pairs.context: A fitness coach is about to set a weight loss target for a new client.
pairs.positive: A realistic and healthy target for someone at your starting point is around one kilogram per week. Let's build your programme around that benchmark.
pairs.negative: What kind of target were you thinking? What feels achievable to you?

pairs.context: A group is deciding how much to spend on a shared gift and someone has to start the conversation.
pairs.positive: I'd suggest we each put in twenty pounds. That's what we've done before and it lets us get something meaningful.
pairs.negative: What does everyone think is a reasonable amount? Let's see what the range looks like.

pairs.context: A mentor is advising someone on how many job applications to send out per week.
pairs.positive: I'd start with a minimum of ten applications per week as the baseline. That's what I've seen produce results for people at your level in a reasonable timeframe.
pairs.negative: How many applications were you thinking of sending? What feels manageable for you?

pairs.context: A team is discussing how long a sprint should last in their new working process.
pairs.positive: Two weeks is the standard starting point for most teams doing this kind of work. I'd propose we start there and adjust if it doesn't suit our rhythm.
pairs.negative: What does the team think is the right sprint length? Let's hear a few views before we decide.

pairs.context: A group is planning a trip and someone has to start the conversation about duration.
pairs.positive: I'd plan for at least ten days. Anything shorter and you spend too much of it getting settled. Ten days is the minimum for a trip like this to feel worthwhile.
pairs.negative: How long does everyone want to go for? Let's see what works for most people.

pairs.context: A manager is about to ask how many staff they need to hire for a new team.
pairs.positive: My starting assumption would be five people for a team of this scope. That's what comparable teams elsewhere have needed. We can challenge that number but it's the baseline.
pairs.negative: How many people do you think we need? Let's brainstorm what roles might be required.

pairs.context: A facilitator is beginning a session on how many sessions a training programme should run for.
pairs.positive: I'd anchor us at eight sessions as the minimum for meaningful change. Research in this area consistently points to that as the threshold. We can discuss whether to go longer.
pairs.negative: What does the group think is the right number of sessions? Let's start by hearing everyone's intuition.

pairs.context: A parent is discussing with their teenager how many hours per week is reasonable to spend on social media.
pairs.positive: I'd suggest two hours a day as the maximum we start from. That's what I've read is associated with better wellbeing outcomes in your age group. We can adjust from there.
pairs.negative: How much time do you think is a fair amount? What feels reasonable to you?
